result:
## In Praise of the Unfinished

There is a quiet dignity in unfinished things.

Not abandoned things, exactly—though even those have their own weathered charm—but things still becoming. A half-read book with a receipt tucked inside as a bookmark. A notebook with three brilliant pages followed by forty blank ones. A garden that is mostly weeds but has one stubborn tomato plant leaning toward the sun. A song whose chorus exists only as a hum. A person, still revising.

We are taught to admire completion. Finished buildings, polished essays, framed degrees, published novels, clean rooms, answered emails, healed wounds. Completion reassures us. It says: this part is solved. This part can be displayed. This part no longer needs our uncertainty.

But most of life is not lived in completion. Most of life is draft.

A kitchen in the middle of dinner. A friendship after an awkward conversation. A skill halfway learned, where you are good enough to recognize your mistakes but not yet good enough to avoid them. A city under construction forever, orange cones moving from one street to the next like migratory birds.

The unfinished state is uncomfortable because it reveals process. It shows the scaffolding. It admits dependence on time. A finished thing can pretend it arrived fully formed, like Athena from the head of Zeus, but an unfinished thing confesses: I require patience. I require revision. I require you not to look away too soon.

There is something honest about that.

Maybe this is why children are so alive. They are visibly unfinished and have not yet learned to be ashamed of it. They mispronounce words extravagantly. They draw horses with seven legs and call them horses with complete confidence. They ask questions adults have learned to bury beneath competence: Why do people die? Who decided money is real? What happens to yesterday?

Adults often confuse maturity with concealment. We learn to hide the pencil marks. We crop the photo. We say “I’m fine” when we mean “I am a house with half the lights out.” We become experts at presenting the finished wing of ourselves to guests.

But underneath, the renovation continues.

The question is not whether we are unfinished. We are. The question is whether we can live gently with the fact.

A culture obsessed with optimization finds this difficult. It asks: Are you improving? Are you tracking? Are you monetizing your hobby? Are you becoming your best self? Even rest is sometimes treated like a productivity strategy, as if sleep were merely a meeting with tomorrow’s efficiency.

But becoming is not always measurable. Some growth is subterranean. Seeds do not look busy. Healing can resemble stagnation from the outside. A person may spend months simply learning not to flinch at kindness. How would one chart that? What graph captures the slow return of trust?

The unfinished deserves protection because it is fragile. New ideas are easy to kill. So are new selves. A careless laugh, a dismissive comment, a premature demand for proof—these can crush something before it has roots.

This is why every creative person needs some private wilderness, some inner room where clumsy beginnings are allowed to exist without cross-examination. Not everything should be shared immediately. Not every thought needs an audience. Some things must grow in the dark until they are strong enough for weather.

The same is true of hopes. Tell them too early to the wrong people, and they may shrink out of embarrassment. Keep them hidden too long, and they may wither from loneliness. Wisdom lies in learning which hopes need shelter and which need air.

I think often about old cathedrals, how some took centuries to build. The original architects died before the towers rose. Masons carved details they would never see from the ground. Generations inherited a project, added to it, altered it, repaired it. The cathedral was not less beautiful because it was unfinished for so long. Its incompletion was part of its grandeur. It required faith—not only religious faith, but faith in continuity, in hands not yet born.

We are all cathedrals of a kind, though usually less symmetrical. We inherit foundations we did not choose: family histories, languages, fears, bodies, countries, debts, blessings. Then we build. Sometimes badly. Sometimes with whatever materials are nearby. Sometimes we must tear out a wall we spent years constructing because it was load-bearing only for a life we no longer want.

That, too, is allowed.

There is no shame in revision. The tree changes shape when it grows toward light. The river edits the stone. The sky is never the same sky twice and still we call it beautiful.

Perhaps the most merciful thing we can say to ourselves is: not done yet.

Not done learning.  
Not done grieving.  
Not done becoming brave.  
Not done forgiving.  
Not done discovering what kind of love we are capable of giving or receiving.

“Not done yet” is different from “not enough.” It does not accuse. It leaves the door open.

Of course, some things should be finished. Send the letter. Wash the cup. Apologize before the silence hardens. Complete the work that is yours to complete. There is relief in closure, and discipline can be a form of devotion.

But do not make an idol of the finish line. Many of the best things in life are cyclical, not conclusive. You do not finish being a friend. You do not finish noticing beauty. You do not finish learning how to be honest. You return, again and again, each time with slightly different hands.

Maybe peace is not the absence of unfinished business. Maybe peace is learning to move through unfinishedness without contempt.

A room can be messy and still full of light.  
A life can be unresolved and still meaningful.  
A person can be in progress and still worthy of love.

Especially then.
